zech_visions.title: Return to Me -- The Horsemen Among the Myrtles
zech_visions.synopsis: Zechariah opens with a call to repentance: 'Return to me, says YHWH of hosts, and I will return to you' (1:3). Then the first night vision: a man riding a red horse stands among the myrtle trees in a ravine, with red, sorrel, and white horses behind him. These are YHWH's patrol -- heavenly riders who have been sent to 'patrol the earth' (1:10). They report to the Angel of YHWH: 'We have patrolled the earth, and behold, all the earth remains at rest' (1:11). The Angel of YHWH then intercedes for Jerusalem: 'O YHWH of hosts, how long will you have no mercy on Jerusalem?' (1:12). YHWH responds with 'gracious and comforting words' (1:13) and promises to return to Jerusalem with mercy. The second vision (1:18-21): four horns (representing the nations that scattered Judah) and four craftsmen sent to cast them down.

key_verse.text: So the man who was standing among the myrtle trees answered, 'These are they whom the LORD has sent to patrol the earth.' And they answered the angel of the LORD who was standing among the myrtle trees, and said, 'We have patrolled the earth, and behold, all the earth remains at rest.'

original_terms: mal'akh YHWH (Angel/Messenger of YHWH -- the heavenly figure who guides Zechariah's visions and intercedes for Jerusalem)
lehithalekh ba-arets (to patrol the earth -- the heavenly horsemen's reconnaissance mission; cf. Job 1:7 where the satan also patrols)
shuvu elai (return to me -- the covenant invitation; YHWH's return depends on their return)
zech_visions.ane_backdrop: The image of mounted riders patrolling an empire was familiar in the Persian period. Darius I's famous mounted couriers (the angarioi) maintained communication across the vast Persian Empire. Zechariah's vision transposes this to the heavenly realm: YHWH has his own mounted patrol surveying the earth.

second_temple.summary: The four horsemen of the Apocalypse: riding white, red, black, and pale horses.
second_temple.relevance: Revelation's four horsemen draw on Zechariah's heavenly riders (1:8; 6:1-8), transforming patrol agents into agents of judgment.

cross_refs.note: The satan 'going to and fro on the earth' -- the same patrol language as Zechariah's horsemen
'The eyes of YHWH range throughout the earth' -- the theological basis for the patrol
The seventy years of exile -- the background for the Angel's 'how long?' intercession
The four horsemen -- drawing on Zechariah's mounted patrol imagery

zech_visions.divine_council_note: Zechariah 1 is a vivid divine council scene. The heavenly horsemen are council agents sent on reconnaissance -- they patrol the earth and report back to the Angel of YHWH, who then intercedes before YHWH. This is the divine council intelligence network in operation. The Angel of YHWH functions as both council mediator (receiving reports from patrol agents) and intercessor (pleading Jerusalem's case before YHWH). The parallel with Job 1:6-7 is striking: in Job, the satan patrols the earth and reports to the council; here, YHWH's loyal agents do the same. The four horns and craftsmen (1:18-21) represent the divine council's judgment on the nations that scattered YHWH's people.

sections.heading: Return to Me (1:1-6)
sections.body: Zechariah's opening oracle arrives in the eighth month of Darius's second year (November 520 BC), two months after Haggai began his Temple-building campaign. The word is blunt: 'YHWH was very angry with your fathers' (1:2). The exile happened because the previous generation refused to heed the former prophets' call to 'return (shuv) from your evil ways and from your evil deeds' (1:4). The verb shuv ('return, repent, turn back') is the key word: YHWH offers a symmetrical covenant: 'Return to me, says YHWH of hosts, and I will return to you' (1:3). The condition is repentance; the promise is YHWH's own return -- his presence among his people. Zechariah adds a sobering observation: 'Your fathers, where are they? And the prophets, do they live forever?' (1:5). Both the disobedient generation and the prophets who warned them are dead. But YHWH's words and decrees 'overtook your fathers' (1:6) -- the prophetic word outlives both prophet and audience. History vindicated every warning.

sections.heading: The First Vision: Horsemen Among the Myrtles (1:7-17)
sections.body: Three months later (February 519 BC), the night visions begin. Zechariah sees a man riding a red horse, standing among the myrtle trees in a ravine (metsulah, suggesting a deep, shadowed place). Behind him are red, sorrel, and white horses with riders. The angel-interpreter explains: 'These are they whom YHWH has sent to patrol the earth' (1:10). This is the divine council's intelligence network -- supernatural agents deployed on reconnaissance throughout the earth, reporting back to command. The patrol reports: 'All the earth remains at rest (shaqat)' (1:11). But this 'rest' is not good news -- it means the nations that destroyed Jerusalem are prospering while YHWH's people suffer in a ruined city. The Angel of YHWH erupts in intercession: 'O YHWH of hosts, how long will you have no mercy on Jerusalem and the cities of Judah, against which you have been angry these seventy years?' (1:12). YHWH responds with 'gracious and comforting words' and declares: 'I am exceedingly jealous (qana) for Jerusalem' and 'very angry with the nations that are at ease' (1:14-15). YHWH allowed punishment, but the nations exceeded their mandate. The temple will be rebuilt. YHWH's measuring line will stretch over Jerusalem.

sections.heading: The Second Vision: Four Horns and Four Craftsmen (1:18-21)
sections.body: Zechariah lifts his eyes and sees four horns (qarnayin) -- in the ancient Near East, horns symbolize power, especially military-political power. These are 'the horns that have scattered Judah, Israel, and Jerusalem' (1:19) -- the empires that dismembered YHWH's people. Then four craftsmen (charashim) appear. 'What are these coming to do?' Zechariah asks. The answer: they have come 'to terrify them, to cast down the horns of the nations who lifted up their horns against the land of Judah to scatter it' (1:21). The divine council deploys agents of judgment against the very empires that served as instruments of discipline. Every horn of power raised against YHWH's people will be broken by a craftsman YHWH commissions. The vision establishes a principle that runs through all of Zechariah: the nations that exceeded their divine mandate in punishing Israel will themselves face judgment. YHWH uses empires, but he does not approve their cruelty.

zech_visions.title: The Measuring Line -- Jerusalem Without Walls
zech_visions.synopsis: The third night vision: a man with a measuring line goes to measure Jerusalem. An angel intercepts him: 'Jerusalem shall be inhabited as villages without walls, because of the multitude of people and livestock in it. And I will be to her a wall of fire all around, declares YHWH, and I will be the glory in her midst' (2:4-5). The population will be so vast that no physical wall can contain it. YHWH himself will be the wall -- a wall of fire and the glory within. Then the call to flee from Babylon: 'Escape to Zion, you who dwell with the daughter of Babylon' (2:7). The promise: 'I am coming and I will dwell in your midst, declares YHWH. And many nations shall join themselves to YHWH in that day, and shall be my people' (2:10-11). This is the inclusion of the Gentiles -- nations streaming to YHWH, the reversal of Deuteronomy 32's allotment.

key_verse.text: Sing and rejoice, O daughter of Zion, for behold, I come and I will dwell in your midst, declares the LORD. And many nations shall join themselves to the LORD in that day, and shall be my people. And I will dwell in your midst, and you shall know that the LORD of hosts has sent me to you.

original_terms: chevel middah (measuring line -- the surveyor's tool; but YHWH's Jerusalem cannot be measured)
chomat esh (wall of fire -- YHWH himself as Jerusalem's protection; theophanic fire imagery)
nilvu (shall join themselves -- the nations will attach themselves to YHWH; the ingathering of the Gentiles)
zech_visions.ane_backdrop: Measuring cities was standard practice for fortification planning in the ancient Near East. But YHWH's Jerusalem cannot be contained by walls -- it will overflow all human planning. The 'wall of fire' echoes the pillar of fire that protected Israel in the wilderness (Exod 13:21-22).

second_temple.summary: An angel measures the new Jerusalem with a measuring rod, but there is no temple in it -- 'for its temple is the Lord God the Almighty and the Lamb.'
second_temple.relevance: Revelation's measured-yet-wall-less Jerusalem echoes Zechariah's vision of a city whose glory exceeds all physical boundaries.

cross_refs.note: The pillar of fire -- YHWH as protective fire, echoed in the 'wall of fire'
Nations streaming to Zion -- the same vision of Gentile inclusion
The allotment of nations to the sons of God -- Zechariah 2:11 reverses this
Christ 'has broken down the dividing wall' -- the ultimate wall-less community
The new Jerusalem -- no temple, no walls, YHWH and the Lamb are the temple

zech_visions.divine_council_note: Zechariah 2:10-11 announces the reversal of the Deuteronomy 32 allotment: 'many nations shall join themselves to YHWH in that day and shall be my people.' The nations that were allotted to the sons of God (Deut 32:8-9) will be reclaimed by YHWH himself. The phrase 'YHWH of hosts has sent me to you' (2:11) involves a mysterious 'sent one' -- the Angel of YHWH speaks as YHWH yet is sent by YHWH, the 'two powers in heaven' pattern that is central to divine council Christology.

sections.heading: The Third Vision: Measuring Jerusalem (2:1-5)
sections.body: A young man goes out with a measuring line to determine Jerusalem's dimensions -- presumably to plan walls for the rebuilt city. An angel intercepts him with a message that demolishes conventional city-planning: 'Jerusalem shall be inhabited as villages without walls, because of the multitude of people and livestock in it' (2:4). The city will be too vast, too overflowing with life, for walls to contain it. Then the extraordinary promise: 'I will be to her a wall of fire all around, declares YHWH, and I will be the glory in her midst' (2:5). YHWH replaces stone walls with fire -- the same fire of the theophany at Sinai (Exod 19:18), the pillar of fire in the wilderness (Exod 13:21), and the glory-fire that filled Solomon's Temple (1 Kings 8:10-11). The city's defense is not military architecture but divine presence. The glory (kavod) that departed the Temple in Ezekiel's vision (Ezek 10:18-19) will return and become Jerusalem's center. No human fortification can contain what YHWH plans. This vision anticipates the new Jerusalem of Revelation 21, which also has no temple or walls because YHWH and the Lamb are its temple and light.

sections.heading: Flee from Babylon -- YHWH Is Coming (2:6-13)
sections.body: The vision transitions to urgent command: 'Up! Up! Flee from the land of the north, declares YHWH, for I have spread you abroad as the four winds of the heavens' (2:6). Jews still living in Babylon must escape before YHWH strikes. Then the stunning declaration: 'He who touches you touches the apple of his eye' (2:8) -- YHWH identifies so closely with his people that an attack on them is an attack on his own eye. YHWH will 'shake his hand' over the oppressor nations, and they will become plunder for their own slaves (2:9). The climax is christologically dense: 'Sing and rejoice, O daughter of Zion, for behold, I come and I will dwell in your midst, declares YHWH. And many nations shall join themselves to YHWH in that day, and shall be my people' (2:10-11). The 'I come' is YHWH himself -- yet the next phrase says 'YHWH of hosts has sent me to you' (2:11). A figure who speaks as YHWH is sent by YHWH -- the 'two powers in heaven' pattern of divine council Christology. Many nations joining YHWH reverses the Deuteronomy 32:8 allotment. The oracle closes: 'Be silent, all flesh, before YHWH, for he has roused himself from his holy dwelling' (2:13) -- the sovereign of the council rises from his throne to act.

zech_visions.title: Satan Accuses Joshua -- The Divine Courtroom
zech_visions.synopsis: The fourth night vision is the most explicit divine council courtroom scene in the OT. Joshua the high priest stands before the Angel of YHWH, clothed in filthy garments. 'The satan' (ha-satan -- the accuser, with the article, indicating a role rather than a proper name) stands at Joshua's right hand to accuse him. YHWH rebukes the accuser: 'YHWH rebuke you, O satan! YHWH who has chosen Jerusalem rebuke you! Is not this a brand plucked from the fire?' (3:2). Joshua's filthy garments are removed ('I have taken your iniquity away from you') and replaced with clean garments and a pure turban. Then YHWH declares: 'Hear now, O Joshua the high priest... behold, I will bring my servant the Branch' (3:8). The stone with seven eyes is set before Joshua: 'I will engrave its inscription, declares YHWH of hosts, and I will remove the iniquity of this land in a single day' (3:9). This is the most christologically dense vision in Zechariah: the accuser silenced, guilt removed, the Branch promised, and iniquity erased 'in a single day' -- Good Friday.

key_verse.text: Hear now, O Joshua the high priest, you and your friends who sit before you, for they are men who are a sign: behold, I will bring my servant the Branch. For behold, on the stone that I have set before Joshua, on a single stone with seven eyes, I will engrave its inscription, declares the LORD of hosts, and I will remove the iniquity of this land in a single day.

original_terms: ha-satan (the accuser/adversary -- with the article; a prosecutorial role in the divine council, not yet a proper name)
tsemach (Branch -- the messianic title; the shoot from David's line; cf. Isa 11:1, Jer 23:5)
tso'im (filthy garments -- not merely dirty but excrement-stained; representing deep moral defilement)
tsanif tahor (clean turban -- the high priest's turban restored; priestly access to God renewed)
zech_visions.ane_backdrop: Divine courtroom scenes were common in Mesopotamian literature. The gods could bring charges against humans or each other. The unique element in Zechariah 3 is the outcome: the accused is not condemned but acquitted and re-clothed. Grace in the divine courtroom is distinctively Israelite theology.

second_temple.summary: 'The accuser of our brothers has been thrown down, who accuses them day and night before our God.'
second_temple.relevance: Revelation's identification of Satan as the cosmic accuser draws directly on Zechariah 3's courtroom scene. The accuser is ultimately silenced.

second_temple.summary: 'Who shall bring any charge against God's elect? It is God who justifies. Who is to condemn?'
second_temple.relevance: Paul's courtroom language echoes the divine council scene: the accuser is rebuked, and God's chosen are acquitted.

God takes his place in the divine council; he holds judgment -- the judicial setting
The Branch (netser) from Jesse's roots -- the same messianic concept
'I will raise up for David a righteous Branch' -- tsemach in Jeremiah's messianic prophecy
'Who shall bring any charge?' -- the divine courtroom acquittal
The accuser thrown down -- the final silencing of the satan

zech_visions.divine_council_note: Zechariah 3 is the most explicit divine council courtroom scene in the OT, surpassing even Job 1-2 in its theological detail. The Angel of YHWH presides as judge. The satan (ha-satan, with the article) occupies the prosecutorial role -- standing at the accused's right hand, the traditional position for an accuser (Ps 109:6). Joshua represents not only himself but Israel: the filthy garments are the nation's defilement. YHWH's rebuke of the accuser is a sovereign decree: the prosecution is dismissed. The re-clothing is judicial acquittal made visible. The 'Branch' announcement (3:8) is a divine council decree: YHWH will send his messianic agent. The removal of iniquity 'in a single day' (3:9) points to the cross, where the ultimate divine council verdict -- guilty humanity acquitted through the Branch's sacrifice -- is enacted.

sections.heading: The Courtroom Scene: Joshua Accused (3:1-3)
sections.body: The fourth night vision opens on a heavenly courtroom. Joshua the high priest -- the nation's representative before God -- stands before the Angel of YHWH, who presides as judge. At Joshua's right hand stands ha-satan ('the adversary'), not yet a proper name but a prosecutorial title with the definite article, identifying a role within the divine council: the accuser, the one who brings charges. Joshua is clothed in 'filthy garments' (begadim tso'im) -- the Hebrew tso'im refers not to ordinary dirt but to excrement-stained clothing, indicating deep moral and ritual defilement. Joshua's filthy garments represent the whole nation's condition: Israel has returned from exile outwardly, but the people remain spiritually contaminated, their priesthood compromised. The scene is a heavenly trial: the highest representative of YHWH's covenant people faces formal prosecution in the very council chamber of God. The accuser has a legitimate case -- Israel's defilement is real, the priestly corruption documented by Malachi is evident, and the covenant violations that caused the exile have not been fully addressed.

sections.heading: The Rebuke and the Re-Clothing (3:4-5)
sections.body: YHWH's response to the prosecution is not a legal defense but a sovereign decree: 'YHWH rebuke you, O satan! YHWH who has chosen Jerusalem rebuke you! Is not this a brand plucked from the fire?' (3:2). The accuser's case may be technically valid, but YHWH overrules it on the basis of his election -- he has chosen Jerusalem, and the accused is a survivor snatched from the fire of exile. YHWH then commands those standing before him (the council attendants): 'Remove the filthy garments from him' (3:4). To Joshua directly: 'Behold, I have taken your iniquity away from you, and I will clothe you with pure vestments.' A clean turban (tsanif tahor) is placed on his head -- the priestly turban that bore the inscription 'Holy to YHWH' (Exod 28:36-38). This is divine acquittal enacted as a visible drama: guilt is not merely forgiven but physically removed. The filthy garments come off; the clean garments go on. The priesthood is not reformed but reconstituted from above. No human effort achieves this purification -- it is entirely YHWH's initiative, YHWH's action, YHWH's gift. The pattern prefigures the New Testament doctrine of justification: the guilty are clothed in righteousness not their own.

sections.heading: The Branch and the Single Day (3:6-10)
sections.body: YHWH charges the restored Joshua: 'If you will walk in my ways and keep my charge, then you shall rule my house and have charge of my courts, and I will give you the right of access among those who are standing here' (3:7) -- 'those standing here' are the divine council members. Joshua is offered a seat in the heavenly assembly. Then the messianic announcement: 'Hear now, O Joshua the high priest, you and your friends who sit before you, for they are men who are a sign (mofet): behold, I will bring my servant the Branch (tsemach)' (3:8). The Branch is the messianic shoot from David's line (cf. Isa 11:1; Jer 23:5; 33:15) -- YHWH's servant who will fulfill what the current priesthood can only foreshadow. A stone with seven eyes is set before Joshua: 'I will engrave its inscription, declares YHWH of hosts, and I will remove the iniquity of this land in a single day (beyom echad)' (3:9). The seven eyes represent YHWH's omniscient gaze (cf. 4:10; 2 Chr 16:9). The 'single day' of total iniquity removal points beyond anything Yom Kippur could accomplish -- it points to the cross, where the Branch accomplishes in one day what the entire sacrificial system spread across centuries could not. The chapter closes with messianic peace: 'In that day, declares YHWH of hosts, every one of you will invite his neighbor to come under his vine and under his fig tree' (3:10) -- the same image from Micah 4:4 and 1 Kings 4:25.

zech_visions.title: The Lampstand and Two Olive Trees -- Not by Might But by My Spirit
zech_visions.synopsis: The fifth night vision: a golden lampstand (menorah) with seven lamps and two olive trees flanking it. The interpreting angel explains: 'This is the word of YHWH to Zerubbabel: Not by might, nor by power, but by my Spirit, says YHWH of hosts' (4:6). The mountain of opposition before Zerubbabel will become a plain. He who laid the foundation will also complete the Temple. 'The seven lamps are the eyes of YHWH, which range through the whole earth' (4:10). The two olive trees are 'the two anointed ones (literally: sons of fresh oil) who stand by the Lord of the whole earth' (4:14). These two figures -- likely Zerubbabel (royal) and Joshua (priestly) -- stand in the divine council before YHWH, channeling his sustaining Spirit to the lampstand (Israel). The vision reveals that the Temple rebuilding is sustained not by human resources but by divine power flowing through YHWH's anointed agents.

key_verse.text: Then he said to me, 'This is the word of the LORD to Zerubbabel: Not by might, nor by power, but by my Spirit, says the LORD of hosts.'

original_terms: menorah (lampstand -- the golden lampstand; symbol of YHWH's presence and Israel's witness)
ruach (Spirit -- YHWH's Spirit, the divine power that accomplishes what human might cannot)
bene ha-yitshar (sons of fresh oil -- the two anointed figures who stand before the Lord of all the earth)
sheva einei YHWH (seven eyes of YHWH -- the lamps representing YHWH's all-seeing gaze ranging through the earth)
zech_visions.ane_backdrop: Lampstands were central to temple furnishing throughout the ancient Near East. In Israel, the menorah symbolized YHWH's presence (Exod 25:31-40). The olive oil connection is practical (olive oil fueled lamps) and theological (anointing oil consecrated priests and kings).

second_temple.summary: 'I will grant authority to my two witnesses... These are the two olive trees and the two lampstands that stand before the Lord of the earth.'
second_temple.relevance: Revelation directly draws on Zechariah's two olive trees/anointed ones, recasting them as the eschatological witnesses.

cross_refs.note: The original menorah design -- the pattern for Zechariah's vision
Ten lampstands in Solomon's Temple -- the furnishing Zechariah's vision recalls
'The eyes of YHWH range through the whole earth' -- the seven eyes theology
Christ among the seven lampstands -- the churches as lampstands drawing on Zechariah
The two witnesses as the two olive trees -- direct Zechariah allusion

zech_visions.divine_council_note: Zechariah 4 reveals the divine council's sustaining infrastructure. The seven lamps are 'the eyes of YHWH, which range through the whole earth' (4:10) -- the divine council's omniscient surveillance, the same concept as the patrol horsemen of chapter 1. The two olive trees/anointed ones 'stand by the Lord of the whole earth' (4:14) -- they occupy positions in the divine council, standing before YHWH's throne. The oil flowing from the olive trees to the lampstand represents the Spirit flowing from YHWH through his anointed agents to sustain Israel. The key declaration -- 'Not by might, nor by power, but by my Spirit' -- is a divine council principle: human empires rise and fall by military power, but YHWH's kingdom operates by his Spirit working through his council.

sections.heading: The Fifth Vision: The Golden Lampstand (4:1-5)
sections.body: The interpreting angel rouses Zechariah 'as a man who is awakened out of his sleep' (4:1) -- the visionary trance has deepened to the point where returning to prophetic consciousness feels like waking. What he sees is extraordinary: a golden lampstand (menorah) with a bowl on top, seven lamps on it, and seven lips (channels) for each lamp -- forty-nine oil conduits total. Two olive trees flank the lampstand, one on the right and one on the left. The imagery is of a self-sustaining, continuously fed light source: living olive trees pour oil directly into the bowl that feeds the lamps. No human priest needs to trim the wicks or refill the oil. The menorah in the Tabernacle and Temple required daily priestly tending (Exod 27:20-21; Lev 24:2-4); this menorah tends itself. The vision declares that YHWH's presence and witness (symbolized by the lampstand) will be sustained by his own power flowing through his appointed agents, not by human religious maintenance. Zechariah asks, 'What are these, my lord?' The angel's response -- 'Do you not know?' -- suggests the symbolism should be recognizable from Israel's liturgical tradition.

sections.heading: Not by Might -- The Word to Zerubbabel (4:6-10)
sections.body: The central oracle of the vision is addressed to Zerubbabel, the Davidic governor leading the Temple reconstruction: 'Not by might (chayil -- military force), nor by power (koach -- human strength), but by my Spirit (ruach), says YHWH of hosts' (4:6). This is the divine council's operating principle: human empires build by military conquest and forced labor; YHWH's kingdom is built by his Spirit working through his agents. The 'great mountain' of opposition before Zerubbabel -- political resistance from Samaria, economic scarcity, community discouragement -- 'shall become a plain' (4:7). Zerubbabel will bring forth the capstone (even ha-roshah, the head stone, the final piece completing the Temple) with shouts of 'Grace, grace to it!' (chen, chen) -- the building's completion is not a human achievement but a divine gift acknowledged with acclamation. YHWH's challenge to the discouraged community: 'For whoever has despised the day of small things shall rejoice, and shall see the plumb line in the hand of Zerubbabel' (4:10). The 'seven eyes' are identified as 'the eyes of YHWH, which range through the whole earth' -- the seven lamps represent YHWH's omniscient gaze, monitoring and sustaining the project from the divine council.

sections.heading: The Two Anointed Ones (4:11-14)
sections.body: Zechariah presses for the meaning of the two olive trees flanking the lampstand. His persistence is notable -- he asks twice, and the angel's response is one of the most theologically significant identifications in the prophetic corpus: 'These are the two sons of fresh oil (bene ha-yitshar) who stand by (omedim al) the Lord of the whole earth (Adon kol-ha-arets)' (4:14). The phrase 'stand by' is technical divine council language -- these figures occupy positions in YHWH's presence, standing before his throne as servants and agents. The 'two sons of fresh oil' are anointed figures -- the royal (Zerubbabel) and priestly (Joshua) offices that together channel YHWH's sustaining power to Israel. The immediate reference is to the post-exilic leadership; the typological significance points to the ultimate king-priest who will unite both offices (Zech 6:12-13; Heb 7). Revelation 11:3-4 directly appropriates this image: 'These are the two olive trees and the two lampstands that stand before the Lord of the earth.' The vision's core theology: YHWH sustains his people through anointed agents who stand in the divine council and mediate his Spirit to the community.

zech_visions.title: Flying Scroll, Woman in the Basket, Four Chariots, and the Crown
zech_visions.synopsis: Chapters 5-6 contain the final three night visions and the crowning of Joshua. The sixth vision (5:1-4): a flying scroll, 30 by 15 feet, containing curses against thieves and perjurers -- YHWH's covenant enforcement document. The seventh vision (5:5-11): a woman named 'Wickedness' is sealed in an ephah (measuring basket) and carried by two winged women to Shinar (Babylon) -- evil is being removed from the land and returned to its source. The eighth vision (6:1-8): four chariots with horses (red, black, white, dappled) go out from between two mountains of bronze. These are 'the four spirits/winds of heaven, going out after presenting themselves before the Lord of all the earth' (6:5). The black horses going north have 'set my Spirit at rest in the north country' (6:8) -- YHWH's judgment on Babylon is accomplished. Then the symbolic act (6:9-15): a crown is made and placed on Joshua's head: 'Behold, the man whose name is the Branch: he shall branch out from his place, and he shall build the temple of YHWH... and he shall bear royal honor, and shall sit and rule on his throne. And there shall be a priest on his throne, and the counsel of peace shall be between them both' (6:12-13).

key_verse.text: And say to him, 'Thus says the LORD of hosts, "Behold, the man whose name is the Branch: for he shall branch out from his place, and he shall build the temple of the LORD. It is he who shall build the temple of the LORD and shall bear royal honor, and shall sit and rule on his throne. And there shall be a priest on his throne, and the counsel of peace shall be between them both."'

original_terms: megillah afah (flying scroll -- the airborne covenant document; YHWH's judgment published in the heavens)
ha-rishah (the Wickedness -- personified evil, female; sealed and exiled to Shinar/Babylon)
arba ruchot ha-shamayim (four spirits/winds of heaven -- the divine council agents dispatched from YHWH's throne)
tsemach (Branch -- the messianic title repeated; the king-priest who will build YHWH's Temple)
zech_visions.ane_backdrop: Shinar (Babylon) was the traditional land of rebellion (Gen 11:2 -- Babel). Sending wickedness back to Shinar is a theological statement: evil returns to its origin. The four chariots emerging from between bronze mountains echo the cosmic mountain traditions of the ancient Near East -- the mountains of the gods from which divine decrees go forth.

second_temple.summary: Jesus, a high priest forever after the order of Melchizedek -- king and priest united.
second_temple.relevance: Zechariah 6:12-13's vision of the king-priest on one throne finds its fulfillment in Jesus, who unites royal and priestly offices.
